stimulus: Plant biologists Diego Salgado, Mei-Lin Chao, and Robert Tanaka grew seedlings of Brassica rapa in samples of simulated Martian regolith and, as a control, in standard greenhouse loam. They found that although most seeds in both groups germinated, the regolith-grown plants developed shorter taproots, accumulated higher levels of anthocyanin pigments in their leaves, and produced fewer rosette leaves overall than the controls. The researchers interpret these symptoms—stunted roots, red coloration, and reduced leaf number—as classic stress responses, suggesting that, while germination on a Mars-analog substrate is possible, sustained growth would require substantial soil amendment.
stem: Which choice best states the main purpose of the text?

answerOptions.content: To discuss the findings of a study evaluating the effects of growing a plant species in Mars-analog soil conditions.
To compare two methods of measuring stress indicators in plants exposed to space radiation.
To describe an experiment that resolved a debate about whether Martian regolith is chemically similar to Earth soils.
To support the claim that no terrestrial plant can germinate in a Mars-analog substrate.

rationale:
Choice A is the best answer. The text walks through the setup, observations, and interpretation of a single study on Brassica rapa grown in Mars-analog regolith, framing its purpose as a report of that study's findings.

Choice B is incorrect because only one method—direct observation of plants—is used; no comparison of methods is undertaken. Choice C is incorrect because the text never frames the work as resolving a soil-chemistry debate. Choice D is incorrect because the text states that most of the seeds did germinate, contradicting the claim in this option.
